mutation-workflow-preview-change-verify.label: Preview, Change, Verify
mutation-workflow-preview-change-verify.summary: Treat every data change as a controlled sequence: identify the target rows, perform one approved mutation, and verify the result.

sketch0.title: From Querying Data to Managing It

sketch1.title: A Safe Workflow for Every Change

sc-mutation-vs-select.title: Difference Between Mutation and Select
sc-mutation-vs-select.prompt: What is the primary difference between a mutation query and a SELECT query in SQL?
sc-mutation-vs-select.hint: Consider what each type of query does to the data.

help.concept: A mutation query changes the data, while a SELECT query only reads it.
help.hint_1: Think about whether the query alters the data.
help.hint_2: SELECT queries do not change data; mutations do.

options.a: Mutation queries change data; SELECT queries do not.
options.b: SELECT queries change data; mutation queries do not.

mc-preview-change-verify.title: Evidence in a Safe Mutation
mc-preview-change-verify.prompt: Which actions provide useful evidence before or after a data change? Select all that apply.
mc-preview-change-verify.hint: Choose the actions that help you identify the target or confirm the result.

help.concept: A safe mutation is supported by evidence: preview the target, review the returned rows, make the approved change, and verify the final state.
help.hint_1: A preview answers which rows are in scope.
help.hint_2: A verification query answers whether the intended result was reached.

dr-mutation-workflow-order.title: Order a Safe Data Change
dr-mutation-workflow-order.prompt: Arrange the actions in the order a careful data change should follow.
dr-mutation-workflow-order.hint: Identify the target before applying the mutation, then inspect the final state.

help.concept: A careful change moves from evidence to action and back to evidence.
help.hint_1: Begin by identifying the intended rows.
help.hint_2: Finish by confirming the database now matches the request.

fb-insert-column-list.title: INSERT Statement Column List
fb-insert-column-list.prompt: In an INSERT statement, you should always include a(n) [blank1] to specify which columns you are inserting values into.
fb-insert-column-list.hint: Think about how to avoid relying on the default column order.

help.concept: An explicit column list makes the mapping between columns and values clear.
help.hint_1: This helps specify exactly where each value goes.
help.hint_2: It prevents errors related to column order.

fb-insert-column-list.template: In an INSERT statement, you should always include a(n) [blank1] to specify which columns you are inserting values into.

mc-verify-mutation.title: Verifying a Mutation
mc-verify-mutation.prompt: Which actions are part of verifying a mutation in SQL?
mc-verify-mutation.hint: Consider what you do after executing a mutation query.

help.concept: Verification involves checking the data to ensure the mutation was successful.
help.hint_1: Think about how you confirm the data has changed as expected.
help.hint_2: Verification often involves running a SELECT query.

options.a: Run a SELECT query to check changes

sc-reset-sandbox.title: Why a Narrow WHERE Clause Matters
sc-reset-sandbox.prompt: Why should an UPDATE or DELETE use the narrowest practical WHERE condition?
sc-reset-sandbox.hint: Consider what happens to rows outside the approved target set.

help.concept: A narrow WHERE clause limits a mutation to the intended records and protects unrelated rows.
help.hint_1: The condition defines the mutation boundary.
help.hint_2: Rows that do not satisfy the condition should remain unchanged.

options.a: It protects unrelated rows from the mutation
options.b: It sorts the affected rows automatically

mutation-workflow-intro.title: What changes when SQL begins to write
mutation-workflow-intro.bodyMarkdown:
Up to this point, most of your SQL work has probably been about asking questions of tables that already exist. You have selected columns, filtered rows, sorted results, combined tables, and shaped reports. This course takes the next step: you will change the database deliberately and design part of its structure yourself.

You will begin with `INSERT`, adding approved rows without disturbing existing data. Next, you will use `UPDATE` and `DELETE` with narrow conditions so that only the intended records change. The second half of the course moves into structure: you will create tables, choose columns and data types, add constraints, and connect related tables with foreign keys. A final capstone brings those skills together in a small relational database.

The workspace stays focused on the current job rather than showing every possible file:

- When a task changes data, `operations.sql` contains the learner-written `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`.
- `query.sql` contains the learner-written preview, inspection, report, or verification query.
- When a task builds database structure, `schema.sql` contains the table definition and constraints.

Some exercises begin with a supplied table and starting rows. That setup remains in the background so you can concentrate on the SQL concept being practiced. A dedicated `seed.sql` file appears only in the capstone, when loading approved starting records is itself part of the task.

The central habit of the course is simple: understand the target, make one justified change, and inspect the result before moving on.

mutation-workflow-steps.title: Preview, change, and verify
mutation-workflow-steps.bodyMarkdown:
Changing data deserves more care than reading it. A `SELECT` can return the wrong rows without altering the database, but an incorrect `UPDATE` or `DELETE` can change many records immediately.

Before changing existing rows, begin with a `SELECT` that uses the condition you plan to apply:

```sql
SELECT id, name, status
FROM inventory_items
WHERE id = 6;
```

Review the result. When it identifies exactly the intended row, reuse the same condition in the mutation:

```sql
UPDATE inventory_items
SET status = 'active'
WHERE id = 6;
```

Then query the row again to confirm the final value:

```sql
SELECT id, name, status
FROM inventory_items
WHERE id = 6;
```

This creates a dependable habit:

1. **Preview** the rows you intend to change.
2. **Change** only those rows.
3. **Verify** the resulting data.

An `INSERT` is slightly different because the new row does not exist yet. Add the approved record first, then select it to confirm that every value was stored in the correct column.

A narrow condition is especially important with `UPDATE` and `DELETE`. Without a suitable `WHERE` clause, one statement can affect every row in the table.
